(Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill||lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} |Shrine rank = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}; formerly {{ill||lt=Prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill||lt=Prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju||WD=|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi||WD=|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju||WD=|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi||WD=|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '埼玉県熊谷市高本', 'language': 'ja'} == Atlas Obscura ID (P7772) == * takagi-shrinehttps://www.atlasobscura.com/places/takagi-shrine {{commons category|Category:Takagi-jinja (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} Hiki |publisher=Saitama Prefecture Shrine Office |year=1992 |ref={{sfnref|Shrines of Saitama|1992}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor= |title=Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia: Cultural Heritage of Our Hometown 11 (Saitama Prefecture) |publisher=Jinbunsha |year=1997 |ref={{sfnref|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library Art and Local History Section, ed. |title=Coloring the History of Kumagaya: Historic Sites, Cultural Properties, and People |publisher=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library |year=2011-3 |ref={{sfnref|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011}} }} === External links === * [http://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/midokoro/takagi.html Takagi Shrine – Kumagaya City Official Website] * He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088|1=Alluvial fan}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606|1=1670}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867|1=Kumagai Naozane}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890|1=Siege of Odawara (1590)}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449|1=Abe Tadaaki}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452|1=Abe Masayoshi}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393|1=Oshi Castle}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}} ; Access * Nearest It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592|1=O-mikuji}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Takamimusubi}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088|1=Alluvial fan}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606|1=1670}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867|1=Kumagai Naozane}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890|1=Siege of Odawara (1590)}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449|1=Abe Tadaaki}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452|1=Abe Masayoshi}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393|1=Oshi Castle}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}) is held. takagi-shrinehttps://www.atlasobscura.com/places/takagi-shrine {{commons category|Category:Takagi-jinja (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592|1=O-mikuji}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Takamimusubi}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088|1=Alluvial fan}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606|1=1670}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867|1=Kumagai Naozane}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890|1=Siege of Odawara (1590)}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449|1=Abe Tadaaki}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452|1=Abe Masayoshi}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393|1=Oshi Castle}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594|1=Kumagaya Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657|1=Takasaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests, ed. |title=Osato District Shrine Register |publisher=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests |year=1930 |id={{NDLJP|1031342}} |ref={{sfnref|Osato District Shrine Register|1930}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests, ed. |title=Osato District Shrine Register |publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai |year=1984 |ref={{sfnref|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Saitama Prefecture Shrine Office Shrine Survey Team, ed. |title=Shrines of Saitama: Osato, Kitakatsushika, Hiki |publisher=Saitama Prefecture Shrine Office |year=1992 |ref={{sfnref|Shrines of Saitama|1992}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor= |title=Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia: Cultural Heritage of Our Hometown 11 (Saitama Prefecture) |publisher=Jinbunsha |year=1997 |ref={{sfnref|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library Art and Local History Section, ed. |title=Coloring the History of Kumagaya: Historic Sites, Cultural Properties, and People |publisher=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library |year=2011-3 |ref={{sfnref|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011}} }} takagi-shrinehttps://www.atlasobscura.com/places/takagi-shrine {{commons category|Category:Takagi-jinja (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592|1=O-mikuji}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Takamimusubi}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088|1=Alluvial fan}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606|1=1670}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867|1=Kumagai Naozane}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890|1=Siege of Odawara (1590)}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449|1=Abe Tadaaki}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452|1=Abe Masayoshi}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393|1=Oshi Castle}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594|1=Kumagaya Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657|1=Takasaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests, ed. |title=Osato District Shrine Register |publisher=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests |year=1930 |id={{NDLJP|1031342}} |ref={{sfnref|Osato District Shrine Register|1930}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests, ed. |title=Osato District Shrine Register |publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai |year=1984 |ref={{sfnref|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Saitama Prefecture Shrine Office Shrine Survey Team, ed. |title=Shrines of Saitama: Osato, Kitakatsushika, Hiki |publisher=Saitama Prefecture Shrine Office |year=1992 |ref={{sfnref|Shrines of Saitama|1992}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor= |title=Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia: Cultural Heritage of Our Hometown 11 (Saitama Prefecture) |publisher=Jinbunsha |year=1997 |ref={{sfnref|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library Art and Local History Section, ed. |title=Coloring the History of Kumagaya: Historic Sites, Cultural Properties, and People |publisher=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library |year=2011-3 |ref={{sfnref|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011}} }} === External links === * [http://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/midokoro/takagi.html Takagi Shrine – Kumagaya City Official Website] * [http://www.takagijinja.com/index.html Musubi no Yashiro Takagi takagi-shrinehttps://www.atlasobscura.com/places/takagi-shrine {{commons category|Category:Takagi-jinja (Kumagaya, Saitama)}} [[JA:高城神社]] {{wikidata link|Q59282644}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takagi Shrine |Image = [[File:Takagi Shrine - panoramio.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}} Miyamachi 2-93 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}; formerly {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Founded = Early Heian period |Main hall = Nagare-zukuri style, copper-plate roofing{{sfn|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984|p=1}} |Annual festival = October 2 |Map = {{Location map|Japan Saitama|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|lat_deg=36|lat_min=8|lat_sec=44|lon_deg=139|lon_min=23|lon_sec=13.1|label=Takagi Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|8|44|N|139|23|13.1|E}}}} }} [[File:Womb-passing ritual Takagi Shrine Kumagaya City Jun 30, 2012 (2).jpg|thumb|160px|Womb-passing ritual]] '''Takagi Shrine''' (たかぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Miyamachi, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592|1=O-mikuji}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Takamimusubi}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088|1=Alluvial fan}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606|1=1670}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867|1=Kumagai Naozane}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890|1=Siege of Odawara (1590)}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449|1=Abe Tadaaki}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452|1=Abe Masayoshi}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393|1=Oshi Castle}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594|1=Kumagaya Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657|1=Takasaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * {{Cite |ref={{sfnref|Shrines of Saitama|1992}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor= |title=Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia: Cultural Heritage of Our Hometown 11 (Saitama Prefecture) |publisher=Jinbunsha |year=1997 |ref={{sfnref|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library Art and Local History Section, ed. |title=Coloring the History of Kumagaya: Historic Sites, Cultural Properties, and People |publisher=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library |year=2011-3 |ref={{sfnref|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011}} }} It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former shrine rank was that of a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. It was regarded as the {{ill|lt=chinju|UNKNOWN|lt=guardian shrine}} of Kumagaya-go{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. It is also known for having “Japan’s longest {{ill|lt=omikuji|Q1069592|WD=Q1069592|1=O-mikuji}}.” === Enshrined deity === * {{ill|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto|1=Takamimusubi}} ** Takamimusubi-no-Mikoto is also known by the alternate name Takagi-no-Kami, and as a deity who personifies generative power, is regarded as a god who creates all things. He is revered as a deity of “matchmaking” and “safe childbirth”{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === History === An ancient shrine recorded in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “One shrine in Ōsato District: Takagi Shrine.” The shrine precincts and surrounding area lie within the {{ill|lt=alluvial fan|Q465088|WD=Q465088|1=Alluvial fan}} of the {{ill|lt=Arakawa River (Kantō)|Q625465|WD=Q625465|lt=Arakawa|1=Arakawa River (Kantō)}}. According to the “Takagi Shrine Origin Record” dated {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|WD=Q7606|1=1670}} ({{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 10), the spring water here was said to be effective against various illnesses, suggesting that a faith centered on this spring existed since ancient times{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the shrine was venerated by {{ill|lt=Kumagai Naozane|Q3335867|WD=Q3335867|1=Kumagai Naozane}}, but it was destroyed by fire during {{ill|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Q187550|WD=Q187550|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}’s {{ill|lt=Odawara Campaign|Q2622890|WD=Q2622890|1=Siege of Odawara (1590)}} in {{ill|lt=1590|Q6764|WD=Q6764|1=1590}} ({{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 18){{sfn|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997|pp=174–175}}. The shrine buildings were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1671|Q7610|WD=Q7610|1=1671}} (Kanbun 11) by {{ill|lt=Abe Tadaaki|Q4666449|WD=Q4666449|1=Abe Tadaaki}} and his son {{ill|lt=Abe Masayoshi|Q10565452|WD=Q10565452|1=Abe Masayoshi}}, lords of {{ill|lt=Oshi Castle|Q1074393|WD=Q1074393|1=Oshi Castle}}{{sfn|Shrines of Saitama|1992|pp=66–71}}. === Womb-passing ritual === * Every year on June 30, a ritual called “womb-passing” ({{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}) is held. It is said that by passing through a chinowa (a ring of thatch) with a diameter of four meters attached to the first torii gate on the approach, one purifies the body and is protected from misfortune{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=139}}. === Annual events and festivals === * January 1 – New Year’s shrine visit: Charms attached to small branches are bestowed, and many people visit from midnight through early morning. * January 15 – Coming-of-Age Ceremony * February 3 – Setsubun Festival * February 11 – National Foundation Festival * April 10 – Spring Mid-Festival * June 30 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}}: see the womb-passing ritual section * October 2 – Annual festival * November 15 – {{ill|lt=Shichi-Go-San|Q1072387|WD=Q1072387|1=Shichi-Go-San}} * December 8 – Thanksgiving Festival and Tori-no-Ichi: The Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine is said to have begun in {{ill|lt=1906|Q2049|WD=Q2049|1=1906}} (Meiji 39){{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kumagaya.keizai.biz/headline/237/|title=“Yōkamachi” Tori-no-Ichi at Takagi Shrine in Kumagaya|work=Kumagaya Keizai Shimbun|publisher=Webuya Co., Ltd.|date=2017-12-08|accessdate=2018-11-26}} and is generally also called Yōkamachi (the Eighth-Day Market). A market is held on the Day of the Rooster, where people purchase various items to welcome the New Year. Over time, many visitors began seeking lucky rakes and household altars, and stalls selling various goods and sweets are set up, creating a lively atmosphere. In Saitama Prefecture, Tori-no-Ichi events begin in Chichibu on December 2, followed by Kawagoe (3rd), Fukaya (5th), Gyōda (6th), and Kumagaya (8th), each with fixed dates. In the case of Takagi Shrine, it is not held on the Day of the Rooster but on the 8th, hence the name Yōkamachi. * December 31 – {{ill|lt=Ōharae|Q11261910|WD=Q11261910|1=Ōharae-shiki}} === On-site information === ; Address * Miyamachi 2-chōme 93, {{ill|lt=Kumagaya City|Q41106|WD=Q41106|1=Kumagaya}}, {{ill|lt=Saitama Prefecture|Q128186|WD=Q128186|1=Saitama Prefecture}} ; Access * Nearest station: About a 10-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kumagaya Station|Q735594|WD=Q735594|1=Kumagaya Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Takasaki Line|Q1346657|WD=Q1346657|1=Takasaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR|1=East Japan Railway Company}}{{sfn|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011|p=138}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests, ed. |title=Osato District Shrine Register |publisher=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests |year=1930 |id={{NDLJP|1031342}} |ref={{sfnref|Osato District Shrine Register|1930}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Osato District Branch of the Saitama Prefectural Association of Shinto Priests, ed. |title=Osato District Shrine Register |publisher=Kokusho Kankōkai |year=1984 |ref={{sfnref|Osato District Shrine Register Reprint|1984}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Saitama Prefecture Shrine Office Shrine Survey Team, ed. |title=Shrines of Saitama: Osato, Kitakatsushika, Hiki |publisher=Saitama Prefecture Shrine Office |year=1992 |ref={{sfnref|Shrines of Saitama|1992}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor= |title=Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia: Cultural Heritage of Our Hometown 11 (Saitama Prefecture) |publisher=Jinbunsha |year=1997 |ref={{sfnref|Local Historical Materials Encyclopedia 11|1997}} }} * {{Cite book|Japanese |editor=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library Art and Local History Section, ed. |title=Coloring the History of Kumagaya: Historic Sites, Cultural Properties, and People |publisher=Kumagaya City Kumagaya Library |year=2011-3 |ref={{sfnref|Coloring the History of Kumagaya|2011}} }} === External links === * [http://www.city.kumagaya.lg.jp/kanko/midokoro/takagi.html Takagi Shrine – Kumagaya City Official Website] * [http://www.takagijinja.com/index.html Musubi no Yashiro Takagi Shrine – Official Website] {{Shinto sidebar}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takagi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takagi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]]
